questions.question_text: A seller's agent learns the buyer plans to offer above list price. Under Iowa Chapter 543B, what must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose this to the seller client
distractors: Keep it confidential from all parties
Share it only if asked by the seller
Advise the buyer to lower the offer
questions.explanation: Under Iowa agency law (Chapter 543B), a seller's agent owes the duty of disclosure to the client (seller). Information about a buyer's willingness to pay more is material and must be communicated. The agent has no duty of confidentiality to the buyer in a seller-agency relationship.

questions.question_text: Which duty does an Iowa licensee owe to ALL parties in a transaction, not just clients?
questions.correct_answer: Honesty and good faith
distractors: Placing the party's interests first
Confidentiality of motivations
Advice on price and negotiation strategy
questions.explanation: Iowa Code 543B.56(1) lists the duties owed to all parties: honesty and good faith, disclosure of known material adverse facts, and accounting for any party's property in the licensee's possession. Placing a client's interests first, keeping motivations confidential, and giving negotiation advice are client-level duties under 543B.56(2) and the commission's agency rules.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent in Iowa discovers structural damage the seller has not disclosed. What is the agent's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the damage to the buyer client
distractors: Wait for the seller to disclose it
Report it only to the listing broker
Disclose it only if the buyer asks
questions.explanation: Under Iowa agency law, a buyer's agent owes the duty of disclosure of material facts to the buyer client. Known structural damage is a material fact that must be communicated promptly, regardless of whether the seller has disclosed it or whether the buyer has asked.

questions.question_text: Under Iowa Code 543B.59, a designated broker appoints different affiliated licensees to represent the seller and the buyer in the same in-house transaction. What is each appointed agent's role?
questions.correct_answer: Each represents only their own client, to the exclusion of other licensees in the firm
distractors: Both automatically become dual agents for both parties
Each owes fiduciary duties to both buyer and seller
The appointment makes the brokerage a subagent of the seller
questions.explanation: Iowa Code 543B.59 lets a designated broker appoint affiliated licensees as appointed agents who exclusively represent different clients in the same transaction. The brokerage and designated broker are not dual agents solely because of the appointment, but a licensee who personally represents both sides is a disclosed dual agent. Clients must receive written notice of the appointed-agent policy before entering a brokerage agreement.

questions.question_text: An Iowa agent representing a seller learns the property has a pending code violation. The agent decides not to mention it to prospective buyers. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: Violation for failing to disclose material facts
distractors: No violation if the seller directed silence
Acceptable if buyers did not ask
Permissible under confidentiality duties
questions.explanation: Iowa law requires all licensees to disclose known material facts to all parties, including adverse information about the property. A pending code violation is material. The duty to disclose material facts to all parties overrides any client instruction to conceal such information.

questions.question_text: When must an Iowa licensee first provide agency disclosure to a consumer?
questions.correct_answer: Before providing specific assistance
distractors: At the time an offer is submitted
When the purchase agreement is signed
Within 48 hours of first meeting
questions.explanation: Iowa law requires agency disclosure at the time the licensee provides 'specific assistance' (Iowa Code 543B.57), and commission rules require the verbal disclosure before specific assistance is provided. A written disclosure acknowledged by separate signatures must follow before any offer is made or accepted. General factual answers about advertised listings and casual price-range conversations do not trigger the duty.

questions.question_text: A prospective buyer calls an Iowa agent about a listing and begins describing her budget, financing preapproval, and why she must move quickly. What obligation has been triggered?
questions.correct_answer: Verbal agency disclosure, because specific assistance has begun
distractors: None, all phone inquiries are ministerial acts
Execution of a buyer agency contract with the caller
Obtaining written consent for dual agency
questions.explanation: Eliciting or accepting a consumer's financial qualifications or buying motives is 'specific assistance' under Iowa Code 543B.57, so the agent must first make the verbal agency disclosure required by commission rule. Merely answering general factual questions about an advertised listing is a ministerial act that triggers no disclosure. A written buyer agreement is not required until the agent shows a property or an offer is to be made.

questions.question_text: An Iowa buyer is ready to submit an offer. Her agent made only a verbal agency disclosure weeks earlier and never obtained a signed written disclosure. Has the agent complied?
questions.correct_answer: No, written acknowledgment is required
distractors: Yes, verbal explanation is sufficient
Yes, if the consumer does not object
Only if a contract is eventually signed
questions.explanation: Iowa uses a two-step disclosure: a verbal disclosure before providing specific assistance, then a written disclosure acknowledged by the separate signatures of the represented parties before any offer is made or accepted. Verbal disclosure alone is sufficient early on, but proceeding to an offer without the signed written disclosure violates Iowa Code 543B.57 and commission rules. If a party refuses to sign, the licensee must document the refusal in the transaction file.

questions.question_text: What must Iowa's written agency disclosure form contain?
questions.correct_answer: Which party the licensee represents and the licensee's statutory duties
distractors: A full explanation of every agency type recognized in Iowa
The commission's schedule of approved commission rates
The names of all licensees affiliated with the brokerage
questions.explanation: Under Iowa Code 543B.57(3), the written agency disclosure must state which party is the licensee's client (or all clients in a dual agency), describe the licensee's duties under 543B.56(1) and (2), and include any clarifying information. Brokers must separately disclose in writing, before a brokerage agreement is signed, the types of brokerage relationships the firm offers — but a broker is not required to offer every type, and there is no commission rate schedule.

questions.question_text: A new Iowa agent provides agency disclosure after helping a buyer tour three homes. Has the agent met disclosure timing requirements?
questions.correct_answer: No, disclosure was required before showings
distractors: Yes, if disclosure was given the same day
Yes, disclosure can occur anytime before offer
No, but only a warning will be issued
questions.explanation: Iowa requires verbal agency disclosure before the licensee provides specific assistance, which touring homes for a buyer client plainly involves. Since July 1, 2024, a written buyer agreement signed by broker and client is also required before any showing. Disclosing only after three tours violates the timing rules regardless of when an offer is eventually made.

questions.question_text: Under Iowa law, when is dual agency permitted?
questions.correct_answer: With informed written consent from both parties
distractors: When the broker verbally informs both parties
Only in commercial transactions
Whenever the same brokerage lists the property
questions.explanation: Iowa permits dual agency only when both the buyer and seller provide informed written consent. Verbal disclosure is insufficient. It applies to all transaction types and does not automatically arise simply because the same brokerage is involved without consent.

questions.question_text: An Iowa dual agent knows the buyer's maximum price is $350,000 on a home listed at $325,000. Without written permission, what may the agent disclose?
questions.correct_answer: Neither party's confidential pricing info
distractors: The buyer's maximum to the seller
The seller's minimum to the buyer
Both parties' pricing to facilitate a deal
questions.explanation: In an Iowa dual agency, confidential information such as maximum price a buyer will pay or minimum price a seller will accept cannot be disclosed to the other party without specific written permission. The dual agent must maintain confidentiality for both sides.

questions.question_text: A dual agent in Iowa actively encourages the buyer to increase their offer to match the seller's expectations. Is this permissible?
questions.correct_answer: No, a dual agent cannot advocate for one party
distractors: Yes, if it helps close the transaction
Yes, if the buyer consented to dual agency
Only if the seller requested it in writing
questions.explanation: Iowa dual agents cannot advocate for or against either party. Encouraging a buyer to raise their offer to meet the seller's expectations constitutes advocacy for the seller. The dual agent must remain neutral and let each party make independent decisions.

questions.question_text: An Iowa seller consents to dual agency, but the buyer refuses. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Assign separate agents or refer one party out
distractors: Proceed as dual agent with seller consent only
Cancel the listing agreement
Default to subagency for the buyer
questions.explanation: Dual agency requires informed written consent from both parties. If the buyer refuses, the broker cannot proceed as a dual agent. The appropriate response is to assign separate agents within the brokerage (if permitted) or refer one party to another brokerage.

questions.question_text: In an Iowa dual agency, the seller tells the dual agent about a foundation crack not visible during showings. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the defect to the buyer
distractors: Keep it confidential as the seller's agent
Disclose it only if the buyer asks directly
Recommend the seller fix it before closing
questions.explanation: Even in dual agency, the duty to disclose material facts applies to all parties. A hidden foundation crack is a material defect that must be disclosed to the buyer. This obligation overrides any confidentiality duty because material defects are never confidential under Iowa law.

questions.question_text: Under the Iowa Real Estate Transparency Act (SF 2291), when must a buyer representation agreement be executed?
questions.correct_answer: Before showing property or before an offer
distractors: Within 30 days of first contact
At the time of closing
Only if the buyer requests representation
questions.explanation: The Iowa Real Estate Transparency Act (SF 2291, effective July 1, 2024) requires a written buyer representation agreement before showing property, or before submitting an offer if no showing occurs. This ensures transparency about the agent's role and compensation from the outset.

questions.question_text: A buyer agent's representation agreement in Iowa does not mention that compensation is negotiable. Does this comply with SF 2291?
questions.correct_answer: No, the agreement must state fees are negotiable
distractors: Yes, if the fee amount is clearly stated
Yes, negotiability is implied by law
Only if the Commission has approved the form
questions.explanation: SF 2291 requires that buyer representation agreements include a conspicuous disclaimer that fees are negotiable and not set by law. Omitting this statement violates the Transparency Act regardless of whether the actual fee amount is disclosed.

questions.question_text: Under Iowa Code 543B.56A, which provision must a buyer representation agreement contain?
questions.correct_answer: A conspicuous statement that compensation is negotiable
distractors: Automatic renewal clause
Exclusive right to all MLS listings
Waiver of the buyer's right to cancel
questions.explanation: Iowa Code 543B.56A(2)(e) requires the brokerage agreement to review the broker's compensation and to conspicuously display a statement that the broker's compensation, fees, and commission are negotiable and not established by law. Automatic renewals, exclusive MLS rights, and cancellation waivers are not required provisions.

questions.question_text: An Iowa buyer wants to see a property listed by her agent's brokerage. The agent has no buyer representation agreement. Under SF 2291, what must happen first?
questions.correct_answer: Execute a written buyer agreement before showing
distractors: Show the property and sign after the tour
Obtain verbal consent and document it later
Have the listing agent show the property instead
questions.explanation: Under Iowa Code 543B.56A(3), a buyer's broker and client must both sign a written buyer agreement before the agent shows a property; the agent cannot show first and formalize later, and verbal consent is not a substitute. The listing agent could show the home to the buyer only as an unrepresented customer — if the buyer wants her own agent to show and represent her, the written agreement must come first.

questions.question_text: Under the Iowa Real Estate Transparency Act, what must a buyer representation agreement state about the agent's compensation?
questions.correct_answer: That fees are negotiable, not set by law
distractors: That fees are fixed by Commission rules
That the seller always pays the fee
That fees follow MLS standard rates
questions.explanation: The Iowa Real Estate Transparency Act (SF 2291) requires buyer representation agreements to include a conspicuous disclaimer that compensation is negotiable and not set by law or the Commission. This ensures buyers understand they can negotiate agent fees.
